title: Internal and External Descriptions of a System
summary: This page introduces the distinction between internal and external descriptions of a system. An external description (input-output relationship) can be obtained from measurements at external terminals without knowledge of internal structure, while an internal description provides complete information about all signals within the system. A circuit example with a capacitor and initial charge is used to illustrate how an external description may not capture all internal behavior.

figures.caption: Armature-controlled dc motor showing (a) the physical setup with field current, armature input x(t), rotor inertia J, damping B, and angular displacement θ(t), and (b) the equivalent mechanical system with applied torque T(t) and viscous damping torque Bθ̇(t).
figures.description: The figure has two labeled subfigures, (a) and (b), with no coordinate axes. In (a), an armature-controlled DC motor is shown with field current i_f, armature input x(t), rotor inertia J, viscous damping B, and angular displacement θ(t) indicated by a rotation arrow on the shaft. In (b), the motor is replaced by an equivalent mechanical rotational model where the input is applied torque T(t) acting on the inertia J, opposed by damping torque Bθ̇(t) and damping coefficient B. Students should notice the correspondence between the electrical motor input in (a) and the simplified torque-driven mechanical system in (b).
